speaker: interviewer
text: [SIDE CONVERSATION]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's June 27, 1995. We're interviewing liberator and nurse, Aldean Mason. The interviewer is Iris Lasky. We're in Woodland Hills, California in the United States, and the interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm Iris Lasky. I-R-I-S L-A-S-K-Y. I'm interviewing Aldean Mason, United States Army nurse. We are in the city of Woodland Hills, California, United States of America. It is June 27, 1995. Aldean, would you start by giving your full name and spell it for?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Aldean E. Mason. A-L-D-E-A-N E. M-A-S-O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Everett, Washington.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is that near?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's on the west coast near Seattle-- near Bellingham, Washington. Snohomish county is where I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your parents and any siblings. Give us their names.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have-- I come from a family of six children-- four brothers and one sister. And I'm the oldest sister. My parents-- my mother was born in Norway, and my dad was born in Sweden. My mother died in 1928, and my dad died in 1931-- '32.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names, your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Sophia, and my dad's name was Fred.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my maiden name is Nelson.

speaker: interviewer
text: Names of your brothers and your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's names are Fred and Edwin, Howard, Robert, and my sister's name is Daphne.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your family like with your religious background? The rel-- religious background was-- I'm-- Protestant religion. Did you attend church?

speaker: interviewee
text: I attended church, the Baptist church.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you believe that your background with your church instilled any particular values in your life as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm sure that it did. Yes. And I'm sure because my parents were deceased early in li-- in my life that the church had some meaning, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any specific goals as a young person growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I think my specific goal was to grow up. [LAUGHS] And to be able, when I went through-- was to go through junior high school and through high school and have some profession so that I would be able to take care of myself. And that's what I hoped for, and, and that's what I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever aware of any racial discrimination or religious discrimination in your town of Everett?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really believe so. I was Scandinavian, and there were many other Scandinavians there, but there are many other nationalities too. And I think that we all seemed to melted together. I just don't remember of-- just I-- on the top of my head, I don't remember any.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any information with anti-Semitism going on in anywhere in your area?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your education?

speaker: interviewee
text: I graduated from high school, and from there I went to nurse's training at the Everett General Hospital and graduated from training in 1942 and obtained my Registered Nurse certificate. And from there I, I moved to California-- Los Angeles, California-- and had a position of staff nursing-- or staff nurse at Saint Vincent's Hospital. And that was in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were many of your other friends in the medical profession as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my friends? Yes. And, of course, in training-- this-- Pearl Harbor broke out in 1941. And after graduating from training, many of the nurses enlisted in-- not enlisted, but joined the Navy or j-- joined the Army Nurse Corps. And then I felt kind of-- and when I was in Los Angeles and working at Saint Vincent's Hospital, I felt kind of compelled to go into the service. And I had three brothers already in the service. And so I thought that-- I'd kind of tossed it around for a while and then did apply in Los Angeles for application to go into the Army Nurse Corps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a decision of your own? Did anybody persuade you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was strictly my own. Mm-hmm. Yes. And my brother, especially my older brother that was in the service-- in the Army-- and he was over in Europe at the ti-- in Africa at the time, I believe. And he didn't care for that. He couldn't understand why I would go into the service or into the Army. And he was my oldest brother and, of course, he always had an interest in myself and my sister. But that's what I wanted to do, and I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you recall what made you even decide to go into nursing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I first wanted to be a school teacher. And I think that I, I had that idea until about in the seventh grade or so. And in the eighth grade, from then on, I just wanted to be a nurse. I read lovely articles about nursing and saw them with their white caps. And I don't-- I don't believe I glorified them, but I can remember in the ninth grade I'd write my name and put RN. And I wanted to be through high school too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you describe yourself as even a young person as a caretaker?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you describe-- described yourself as a young person as a caretaker? Someone who took care of people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I loved-- yes. I lo-- I loved nursing. And we did take care of patients. And we tried to do our best. I, I loved it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any phase of nursing that you particularly didn't care for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I don't know whether it would be surgery or not, but later on I had some surgery in the Army detached service, and I liked it very much. And maybe-- we were caring for soldiers that came from France, and I thought it was very interesting. But perhaps that might be one of the departments. But I think I was listed also in the s-- for-- in the Army to help in surgery. Although later on, I actually was a ward nurse in, in the Army, other than the one time that I was in England. I was working in surgery detached service.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you applied for the US Army, describe how you did that.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I applied for it, I went down to the recruitment office in the city of Los Angeles. And before that time, I had found out what was the applications to be filled out. And you had to apply to the Red Cross to be a Red Cross, a member of the Red Cross. So that took I don't know just how long to get that membership. Then for the-- to get into the Army, didn't-- wasn't too long of a waiting period. When they read your or had your application, you-- if you were taken, you had to take your physical. And it just seemed like it went very fast, one, two, three. And then I received my order that I was accepted into the Army Nurse Corps, and I'd have 30 days to take care of my duty, or take care of everything, and then would receive orders to go into the Army. And at that time, you had to have a special pass, would you say, to present to the nurse at the hospital where I worked-- the superintendent-- to be released from civilian duty. And so I went to her, and she was very nice. And, of course, they needed nurses in the hospital, but I was released. Then I went-- returned to the state of Washington and waited for my orders.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your orders come, and what were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe they came the 1st of January in 1944. And then I was sent for basic training at-- in Oregon at Camp White. And then from there I was sent to Camp Cooke California, which is near Lompoc, California. And 11th Armored Division was located there at the time waiting-- doing their practice and waiting to go overseas. And so that's where I worked or was in the hospital there, a station hospital. And then I received my orders to go to, to join the 120th Evacuation Hospital Unit in Camp Shelby, Mississippi. And that was in the fall. And it had been-- the 120th Evacuation Hospital had been evacuated-- or activated, excuse me-- in March of 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did you know much about what the evacuation hospital was doing when they were activated? We knew that we'd probably-- we thought we would be really working right up close to the front or in the units overseas, but we were really trained more at Camp Shelby, Mississippi to what to expect. How were you trained?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all we had-- what should I say-- basic training marches. And then we had manuals of, of how to take care of patients, security, would you say, and what not to talk about when we're overseas. And other, other training in diseases and on that order.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of patients do you-- were you trained to treat? Was there any particular type?

speaker: interviewee
text: We really-- we were trained to go overseas and to take care of patients that were wounded. And we had I don't remember just now how many tents and that they would come in. And then we had a setup of a surgery. We had surgeons, x-ray, and which would be set up as an operating room. They would be sent back, I believe, from a field hospital. I'm, I'm not sure of the order now. And then, then we'd send them on to other hospitals or over, say, if we were in France, on to England. But we really-- when we arrived, when we finally went overseas-- received orders from Camp Shelby, Mississippi-- we left on the Queen Elizabeth and landed on-- in Scotland and then went by train to Tenby, Wales, down to southern Wales. And we were billeted in the town of Tenby, Wales. And so while we were there, we didn't know what our orders would be. So there again, the nurses were sent out on detached service. And that's where I went to a-- had my surgery-- sur-- went to a hospital and worked in surgery. Then we were waiting for orders all the time to go to France, and we received orders there. And so, as far as I believe, it was only one time that we were set up that we actually did a dry run. I think it was just one time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Be-- before you did the dry run before you went over to France?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- not before we went to France. No, over in France, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in the detached service, and you were doing surgery, where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was north of London, England. And while we were there and went into London, we-- the, bombs were all around. B2 bombs were around.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you experiencing any fears at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Might have had a few fears, but-- you mean at this time? Maybe later on we had some fears, but we knew the Army would take care of us. I mean, we felt protected. At least I did with the Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know of what was happening in the fighting areas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too much. At the time that we arrived in London, England, we, we really didn't know that the war was progressing as well as it was until we got over to London, England. At least I didn't. What we read in the newspaper, that was-- didn't mean much. And then we knew, like when we were in London and had bombs were flying around. And not until we crossed the-- were shipped from Southampton, England to, to Le Havre-- Le Havre, I believe it was-- France that then we star-- when we unloaded off the ship and got onto trucks, then we saw this drastic war-torn villages and all I-- saw the havoc of war. Then we knew we were-- this was war. And then we went in into different areas there. I believe we were-- went into the Seventh Army at Nancy, France. And so it was war all around. But we still had not set up our hospital. And then from-- we stopped in other places in France, and then we went into Germany and joined the Third Army in Germany, General Patton's army. And we were in Frankfurt, Germany and in a race track. We were-- had our tents set up. And we were still waiting for orders. We didn't know what our mission was going to be. And all around in Frankfurt, Germany were these beautiful homes. We went across from the track, racetrack, but many of them were destroyed. And we saw these war-torn cities there. And then one night we received orders-- not one-- it was in the morning, early morning-- to strike our tents and that we were going to be leaving, and that thousands of our GIs were dying, and that this was where we were going to be going, to take care of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what day that was, or date? Approximately.

speaker: interviewee
text: It would be around the 7th, 7th of April.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after they told you to strike your tents, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had this long drive-- our longest drive-- and we thought we were going to the area where our GIs were. Instead, we went to Buchenwald concentration camp. And pardon me. That's where we-- that was where we were going. We didn't know this in the beginning. But they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Aldean, you mentioned that you were coming into Buchenwald. Please go from there. What was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took several hours then after we struck our-- after the tents were struck in Frankfurt, Germany. And it seemed hours and hours, but as I recall, it was nighttime. I don't know whether 7:00 or 8:00. But I was just-- and as we approached we said, what is this smell? The nurses. And we could hardly stand it, and we got closer and closer, but we didn't know that we were coming right to Buchenwald. And the smell, we-- it was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: And just a month ago, I was talking to Mel Silver from Swansea, Mass-- Mass, and something came up about Buchenwald or coming into it. And he said that he and Herb-- Herb James from Washington state were the-- in the first Jeep that went into Buchenwald. And that Herb said, what is that smell? And he said, oh, that smell is of bodies. He says, those are burnt bodies. And Herb said, what? He said, oh, I have worked in a funeral parlor. His grandfather had a funeral place. He said, I know burned bodies. And so he was in that first echelon of trucks that went in there. So then, then as we approached nearer, we got-- that was the closest that we had been to the front, that our unit had been to the front. And there were infantry men around, and it was dark like I told you. And when we got-- then we found out that we were near Buchenwald, a concentration camp, and we went into Weimar. I believe that was the city. And so then we were set up. The nurses went into this large, lovely manor house, and we took our bedding rolls and slept there. I don't know where the men slept.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then later they put tents down below that area, and we were there for a few days. But the next day or so, then we found out this was our mission was to be at Buchenwald concentration camp and not Army hospital where these thousands of people were-- that we had orders to. We thought thousands of our soldiers had died, but it wasn't that. And so then I th-- I don't know whether it was the next day or a few days later then, that the men were-- they knew they were going to be staying there. But the nurses got to go into the camp and look around at the buildings. Not inside them, but we got to come and view all the bodies that they had stacked in the trucks all around. And got-- we viewed them with the town people. And the nurses would stand around, and we saw these German people. And they had-- some of them were expressionless. And no expression on their face. And like-- and they said they didn't know anything about it. And we took pictures then of them. This was very traumatic, and there were a few nurses that fainted from seeing those bodies. But then we-- then we-- our colonel changed. I don't know. At first we thought maybe we'd be in there working there, but that wasn't the case. We were sent then on detached service to another evacuation hospital. But that smell and the sight that we saw stayed on for a long, long time. And then they-- our officers took as souvenirs some of the lamp shades that they made from human skin. And I don't know if they had shrunk up some of the heads, too, but they had other souvenirs. And a few of them took those home. I couldn't understand that at all. But that was our entrance into Buchenwald concentration camp, and then the men stayed there. I think it was three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you recall your, your initial moment of seeing the bodies in the camp? Your personal moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've never seen anything like it. I wasn't prepared for that in, in my training. I saw many, many patients that died, and we had a procedure of taking care of those patients, and with loving care. When we took care of our own patients in the hospital, we treated them, tried to as a patient, even though they were deceased. And they'd come to pick them up. But I had never seen anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it bring up any particular emotion within you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm sure it brought it up with all of us. We couldn't believe that this was happening there. We knew what was happening to our soldiers, but we couldn't believe this was happening to these people. And then later, like, all we knew was that they said they were political people, political prisoners. Well, then we found out differently. No, I would say it was very traumatic to see it. And then from then on to-- later on, then, when we went on further, and then we'd see these poor emaciated people and go to the-- to our hospital, then we knew all this was true. I mean that we learned then what was happening, and that they were-- the camps were be-- were liberated, and these were people that had been there for-- from one year, two years, and heard the stories.
